I generally dislike relying on invisible string concatenation because it's brittle.  If, somehow, the string no longer exists inside parentheses, say because you pull it out to its own variable name `exp = '....'` then it fails silently.  I've been burned by this kind of thing before, so I try to avoid it.

> Isn't this true of literally every string in Python?

Not strings that could have apostrophes in them (which we don't in this case) or could be interpolated via %/{} (which we also don't).  Maybe it's just my own quirk to use `r''' '''` for all regexps just in case those things do pop up naturally in the expression.

Hey @marcosdiez, @theychx and I discussed this on Gitter, and we agree that eliminating the race condition makes the most sense. However, could you change the code in the check to this?:

```python
def _is_app_ready(self, status):
    if status.app_id != self.app_id:
        return False
    if self.app_id == DASHCAST_APP_ID:
        # Big-ass comment about why this is here here.
        return status.status_text == "Application ready"
    return True
```

That way, we get rid of the confusingly-named class variable that's only used in one place, and we have a comment on the special case explaining exactly why it happens.

Also, could you add a comment on the check in `__init__` on the `CastStatusListener` about why the DashCast check is necessary? I think this is a good compromise between clearly documenting the special-case code and eliminating any potential race conditions.

I was thinking that having the property called `current_time` would make things look more uniform throughout the rest of the code, as well as making any future refactoring easier.

The previous implementation had `self.current_time = time.time()` and `self.start_time = time.time()` sprinkled throughout,

As well as:
`self.current_time = time.time()`
`self.diff_time = time.gmtime(self.current_time - self.start_time)`
and:
`self.saved_time = time.time() - self.start_time`

I thought that having:
`self.saved_time = self.current_time - self.start_time`
would be more consistent and readable.

I was also thinking that we could maybe transition to a different library, like `datetime`, so that we could just use the built-in `datetime.timedelta` instead of calculating it ourselves.  Having it defined in the one place would make it somewhat trivial.

To me the log messages itself are most important feature of previous commits. My personal preference is to have one globally available logger per module. However due to how flask loads extensions it is somewhat complicated to get the logging right. I see one possible problem here - in some rare cases user might end up writing log messages into 2 separate loggers. That's why I would take the path of using one, well known logger name for Flask_CORS. My first approach was to use __name__ attribute, but using "flask.ext.cors" would be even better. Unfortunately there is no standarised way of doing this for Flask extensions :(

> Where would you search if the code does not work? 

In the documentation, I mean seriously. 

Not  *if it does not work*, but if it does not optimize to an optima you are expecting. By that time you would probably check what can be done with the topology. The keyword is to provide **sensible** defaults. In this case our defaults will be the traditional approach, as you've mentioned. :+1: 

Also, look at how `static`is defined: `True` or `False`. Think of this as a switch with a default state (in this case, turned-off). What a user can do is to turn-on a switch so that they get out of that traditional scheme and lock themselves into a fixed set of neighbors. Ok, sure we can change this to  `connection='static'` or `'dynamic'`, but if we have a discrete choice between `1` or `2`, why not just make it `0` or `1`?

> I think in the SVM example you gave, the choice to have default values makes sense because you have to make very complex decisions about the parameters and you have a lot of parameters.

Think of `shrinking`, `probability`, or `class_weights`. I can call SVM, provide C and gamma, and care less about what shrinking does. I trust that it is a sensible default, and I just focus on building the model. If I want to further optimize, then I look at what turning it off means.

Same with `probability`, I know that by setting it to `True`, I sacrifice training speed (according to the docs), I just turn it on when I need it and focus on my model.

> The user has to ask one simple question "static or dynamic?" and that's it. I think even with the active choice of the parameter it still remains very simple as long as we don't add any more.

Again, there's a cognitive overhead. I still need to check for what `static` means or what `dynamic` does. We can provide the traditional default first, and have them change that whenever needed.

> If we strictly follow this philosophy we'd also have to set defaults for the options, bounds, swarm size etc. in the optimizers, wouldn't we?

We already have defaults for bounds. We can keep the swarm size flexible but you're correct to set a default  `options` dictionary. I've been thinking of doing that for some time, but I realized that the common workflow in PSO is that researchers choose values for the cognitive and social parameters and so we follow what they usually do.

> I was actually thinking about profiling the whole GeneralOptimizer class with every topology for v0.4.0 so we can show some examples in the README, what do you think about that? Might be a good first-timers issue as well.

This is a good idea.

> PS: I heard that in many Asian cultures there is a very strong hierarchical structure in projects and that there is little questioning about decisions of the supervisor. This is a very European thing to do. I hope I don't insult you in any way by doing so. Just want this project to become a great product 

All fine, but no need to mention this *explicitly* to be honest. Same with `Pyramid(static)` :laughing: 
We're just open-source enthusiasts, after all :+1: : 

> For exactly the simplicity you're describing we're still keeping the GlobalOptimizer and the LocalOptimizer classes.

Again, simplicity is key. We want the user to use our methods right off the bat. Customization happens *when needed* and not at every step of the way. I'm still trying to find the right balance between this philosophy and flexibility. We're flexible, sure. That's the reason why we try to implement as many options as possible. :100:

The override of `CONDA=1` here prevents a Conda build from running. That means we no longer produce Conda 2.7 packages and no longer run tests on that configuration.

We probably want a separate task for building wheels. That, or we integrate cibuildwheel into each individual job. However, the individual route is a bit more painful because cibuildwheel seems to insist on an opt-out list of platforms instead of an opt-in list. e.g. each individual job would need `CIBW_SKIP=cp27-* cp34-* cp35-*` or some such. That's a bit annoying. Probably easier to just produce the wheels in a separate job. But I worry about doing it that way because the wheels produced with cibuildwheel won't be the wheels used for tests. There is a potential for the build configuration to vary there. So I have to insist on using cibuildwheel in individual jobs and then running tests against the built wheel.

If you refactor this to not break Conda 2.7 builds, I can integrate cibuildwheel into other tasks on my own after it lands.

```
case `uname` in
   Linux)
```

Hm.. I'm not sure that proposed solution is good enough to go with due to number of reasons.

* First, according to [RFC 7159](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7159#page-9), JSON text SHALL be encoded in UTF-8, UTF-16, or UTF-32. Encoding it in `latin1` may break consumers one day.

* Second, what do you expect to see if you pass Python 2 `str` (which is bytes) to `json.dumps()`? Obviously, bytes may contain unserializable characters. Instead, I think it would make more sense to craft a `unicode` (in Python 2) object instead. Can we figure out why exactly `expl` is `str` in py2?

"As far as I can tell, the InclinedExponential calculations need to be redone for each value of h_tani_over_r" - This is correct.

As for how it might be useful, this is my imagined use case: Many simulated galaxies are needed, planned to be generated via photon shooting, with varying inclination angles (but the same h/r). If inclination angles are discretized to the nearest degree, then h_tani_over_r values will end up being reused. This means that the IFT would only need to be calculated 90 times at most (in this example), rather than once per galaxy. This could result in significant time savings.

Granted, this does depend on a few assumptions:
* The user would specifically want to do this with photon shooting
* Discretizing the inclination angle (or h_tani_over_r values) is acceptable
* There isn't a better way to do this

I added the following paragraph:

    The Spergel profile is intended as a generic galaxy profile, somewhat like a Sersic profile, but
    with the advantage of being analytic in both real space and Fourier space.  The Spergel index
    `nu` plays a similar role to the Sersic index `n`, in that it adjusts the relative peakiness of
    the profile core and the relative prominence of the profile wings.  At `nu = 0.5`, the Spergel
    profile is equivalent to an Exponential profile (or alternatively an `n = 1.0` Sersic profile).
    At `nu = -0.6` (and in the radial range near the half-light radius), the Spergel profile is
    similar to a DeVaucouleurs profile or `n = 4.0` Sersic profile.  Note that a `nu = -0.6` Spergel
    profile is roughly 3x faster to draw than an `n = 4.0` Sersic profile, though a `nu = 0.5`
    Spergel profile, `n = 1.0` Sersic profile, and an Exponential profile all take about the same
    amount of time to draw.

There should be python docstrings for these classes and methods. Also, I think subclassing `JSONFieldSerializer` could provide more code reuse. Something like this (untested!):
```python
class JSONFieldSerializer(serializers.Field):
    """
    A Django REST framework serializer for JSON data.
    """

    def to_representation(self, obj):
        """Serialize the field's JSON data, for read operations."""
        return obj

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        """Deserialize the field's JSON data, for write operations."""
        Transform lists of primitive values dicts of native values to
        try:
            val = json.loads(data)
        except TypeError:
            val = data
        return val


class JSONIntFieldSerializer(JSONFieldSerializer):
    """
    A JSON serializer that coerces its data to integers.
    """

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        """Deserialize the field's JSON integer data."""
        field = super(JSONIntFieldSerializer, self).to_internal_value(data)

        for k, v in field.viewitems():
            if v is not None:  # NoneType is used to unset a value
                field[k] = int(v)

        return field


class JSONStringFieldSerializer(JSONFieldSerializer):
    """
    A JSON serializer that coerces its data to strings.
    """

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        """Deserialize the field's JSON string data."""
        field = super(JSONStringFieldSerializer, self).to_internal_value(data)

        for k, v in field.viewitems():
            if v is not None:  # NoneType is used to unset a value
                field[k] = unicode(v)

        return field
```

At least for what my understanding was, and what I outlined in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6228, I had intended to do the magic of automatically adding `defaults` ONLY if no other configuration for channels was supplied.  That included environment variables.

Let's rethink that though.  At least discuss it.  Thinking through it, I still come down on the side of not magically adding `defaults` if ANY other configuration is set for channels.  I think there are two reasons.

1. If a user explicitly sets an environment variable, they probably know enough about what they're doing that they'll include `defaults` if they want it.
2. It becomes much more inconvenient from the CLI perspective to get to the negative case of *not* adding the `defaults` channel.  What I mean, is that, if I really, really don't want `defaults`, but only for this operation, right now I have an option that doesn't force me to modify a file and then modify it back after my operation is complete.  Say I only want conda-forge for the one operation.

        conda config --add channels conda-forge
        conda config --remove channels defaults
        conda install some-package
        conda config --remove channels conda-forge

    or, edit condarc directly, but edit it twice, before and after.

Those are my thoughts anyway.  What are yours?

> The problem is that there is only one generic fit method in which EM steps are conducted and that's why I call _is_identifiable in fit method.

I see two ways to overcome this problem:

1. Add `_is_identifiable` property to `_BaseHMM`. The property should be `True` by default. The subclasses are free to override it and check the parameters for identifiability. This in fact is a slight modification of what you're proposing in the PR. The only difference is that `_is_identifiable` is a property, not a method. 
2. Do not change `_BaseHMM` and add diagnostic step to `_m_step` of the relevant models. The method is expected to issue a warning if parameters aren't identifiable.

The former way implies that EM iterates until it finds identifiable parameters. While in theory this might help with pathological cases, I'm not sure it is very useful in practice. Can you give an example of the dataset where EM first converges to equal means and then after a number of iterations uncovers the true means?

P. S. There's also a problem of defining `equals` for real-valued parameters. For some applications `0.0001` is equal to `0.00012` for other not. And since we are operating in floating point we **have** to pick the threshold somehow.

As it stands the Object is made _empty_ as there is no dictionary associated with schema attributes which will trigger a load in the ```__get__``` method. Returning a vanilla object here will just make the object look like a new GangaObject and never trigger a load from disk until disk access is requested.

I'm much more keen to move to a framework where each object explicitly knows that it's been loaded or not based upon an internal boolean. This avoids all of this conditional object inspection and allows us to simply the question of if the thing ever loaded from the disk or not.
